figures.caption: Figure 8: Qualitative comparison on FFHQ – ×4 super-resolution (noiseless).
Figure 9: Qualitative comparison on FFHQ – ×4 super-resolution (noisy).

Table 8: Robustness to increasing measurement noise on FFHQ. We report PSNR and SSIM for Gaussian and motion deblurring under different noise levels σ. Best values are in bold and secondbest values are underlined.
Figure 1: Overview of the LAMP scheme. Left: relation among the updates of the considered diffusive samplers for a fixed time. Right: examples showing LAMP onto the DDRM method.
Table 1: Quantitative comparison in the noisy setting. Best values are in bold and second-best values are underlined. Errors over this measures can be found in the Table 9.

Table 9: Robustness evaluation in the noisy setting (σy = 0.05) across multiple runs. For each method and task, we report the average PSNR and SSIM, together with the standard deviation computed over R = 5 runs with different seeds, indicated in gray. Best values are in bold and second-best values are underlined.
Table 2: Hyperparameters used by LAMP in the noiseless setting (σy = 0) and noisy setting (σy = 0.05). The parameters µ and ζ are used by DiffPIR, while η and ηB are used by DDRM.
Figure 4: Qualitative comparison on CelebA – Gaussian deblurring (noiseless).
Figure 5: Qualitative comparison on CelebA – Gaussian deblurring (noisy).
Figure 2: Qualitative comparison for noisy motion deblurring across different datasets.

Figure 3: Ablation of the lagged correction strength. Left: PSNR and SSIM as functions of the average coefficient β̄ (with corresponding γ values shown on top). Right: geometric interpretation of the temporal correction, highlighting the role of the coefficient β as in (16) at each temporal update.
Figure 6: Qualitative comparison on ImageNet – Motion deblurring (noiseless).
Figure 7: Qualitative comparison on ImageNet – Motion deblurring (noisy).
Table 7: Quantitative comparison in the noiseless setting. Best values are in bold and second-best values are underlined.
